item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first and third images could not be found under any source as cc-compliant, and the second image comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why it needs to be keter, though I may just not have understood it like some other commenters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many people told you to trim it down and do away with unnecesary over-explanation, you said you would consider it but ultimately did nothing about it. That's ok, it's your scip. Here's one to stubbornness!

Downvoted

Other than that dramatic effect, I'll say this thing relies a little too much on Italian folklore and doesn't quite explain it as it should. The entire redacted interview feels cliché and cheap, it's obviously a copout to create some fear when you weren't able or willing to at least point out at something, anything, man; as it is now, I could guess that the W just stuck his whole open hand up his bum while furiously ad libbing about ways to skin a hunchback with a piece of fleece. You don't leave any clue, just that "Oh, it's so creepy we had to protect you from even knowing! 2spooky4me" overused, tired, trope.

To sum up, this here has potential, but the delivery leaves a lot to be desired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It started off strong, but petered out near the end for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That'd explain it then: everything in said novel is explicitly, absurdly, shamelessly Italian. I'll add Ginzburg to the tally of said shamelessness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, nope - my main source of inspiration were Carlo Ginzburg's Benandantes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this in any way inspired by the Night Walkers of Certando from the novel Tigana? I'm reading through said book for the first time and the similarities are remarkable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it were me, I'd go with the latter option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it might be confusing, but eventually kept it. Would SCP-1623-3(-1 to -4) do it better? Maybe, 1623-3-1 to -4?

This is pretty much an open question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I getcha.

SCP-1623-3 -1, -2, -3 and -4

This is just a tad confusing, then. All I can think to suggest is to connect the -1 to the 3 instead of having the space there, because I parsed it as "SCP-1623-1, -2, -3, and -4." Could just be me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is, it has always been that way - that throughout the centuries, the -1 and -2 had always have only single-digited number of remaining projections. The only way the Foundation is not taking any chances is by keeping an absurd ratio (in this case, a very secure ratio, not taking any chances) of the instances ( one wouldn't expect 1 projection to beat 10 ), so they are very wary with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In case of a sudden disparity (1:1.3 and more) between the -1 and -2 instances, Procedure-1991-Zorutti is to be immediately carried out to even the ratio.

It is theorized that in case of more than ███ projections of either designation remaining and successfuly completing [REDACTED], a possible AK-class scenario (Agricultural breakdown) or UK-class scenario (Uninhabitability) might occur.

Even if the populations are kept close to a 1:1 ratio, through sheer luck one side might get a win that big, and I don't think the Foundation would trust to luck to keep a [_]K scenario from happening. A method which didn't require luck would be to keep each of the -1 and -2 populations below ███.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey TL333s,

glad you like it! 1623-4 doesn't really exist, but 1623-3-4 is one of the fields the skirmishes take place on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Googling "Benandante" when I came across made this really cool. I do have to wonder how they know there won't be warmer days ahead…

Other than a few typos, I'll mention you never explain what 1623-4 is. But I really like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! ^_^ Glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, why did I miss this one…

I like it a lot, MC! Might not be perfect, yadda yadda, but I love the feeling of a magic battle going on during the summer nights. It's, now that I think of it, a Midsummer's Night Battle!

In Italy.

That is awesome.

+1 with a feeling of satisfaction. : 3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comment and the thoughts.

+ Headcanon
- Headcanon

Heck, I even thought of throwing in the GOC as the original disruptors (Didn't, because HI had to do, in my headcanon, something with the old Inquisition which heavily scrutinized Bs and Ws in the past) and 407 as a byproduct of the Benandante's big victores (didn't, felt like it is too much along with the GOC). Glad I didn't.

___

EDIT: Also for the wording fixes. Non-native speaker here. If you don't mind, what did you fix?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the thoughts, it sure is something to think about next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As always, this is all opinion. I'm not a veteran member or staff, so keep that in mind.

What to do with B and W in public? What about the captured ones? What to do, when there is a disproportional number of either? What to do with the area during the event? And then: How? etc., etc.

+ Long Response
- Hide whatever

In a REAL scientific document… you would be right. If the Foundation were real, each SCP would be dozens of pages long. Hell, each Containment Procedure would be at LEAST dozens of pages long, detailing each and every possible permutation, every reaction for any possible action. I know this, because I do technical writing as my job, and I work for a huge government agency.

But the Foundation isn't real, and thus, you don't _actually_ need to prepare for every contingency. You only need to prepare for what you think your readers may mentally call you out on. In my experience here, you basically just need to show that you were thorough and your Foundation is putting enough thought into containing everything.

Specifically:
Your first two sentences BOTH talk about containing the battlefields. You probably only need the first sentence, as the second presents a ton of useless information (is the size of the battlefields important?) and has an SCP acronym which might be a turn-off for some. Next sentence is fine. Then, you don't really need the number of MTF stations built, that's really just implied when you mention MTF at all. Next sentence is fine. But then, do you really need to go into how the Foundation gets its temperature data? Do you need to mention that they have their own temperature satellites? And then you go into a contingency plan about what happens if the satellite is down. Unless this skip has something to do with that satellite and interfering with it, I think you can just get rid of that whole piece and I guarantee you no one will question you. The Foundation has so many assets, you may assume they can get reliable temperature data without anyone questioning you.

The rest is okay, but you might get rid of the tracking from birth portion. It opens too many questions. How do they know for sure who's an instance? Etc?

Just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that I think about it, Soldatesca Cavalleria di Pisa would make more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a solid attempt, but in the end I can't get behind this one. Part of it is that there's a lot of things on the periphery that I feel are distracting. Like the future-predicting satellite that's necessary for containment, or the Horizon Initiative being involved. There's too many moving parts, in my opinion, for the narrative that you're going for. On top of that, you've got an interpretation of the Foundation that seems to involve it being many centuries old, which is interesting to me, but when it's thrown in with all the other stuff on the side you've got going, it feels like yet another distraction.

Conceptually, I find the idea of duelling sets of astral projections fighting and affecting that year's agricultural outlook interesting, but I feel that it loses something in the translation. Like, as a folk legend, I find it really intriguing, and I think it would be really cool to hang out at a tavern somewhere and listen to some old Italian farmer's tales of spiritual battles with crazy famine-imps from his youth. As a scientific report of anomalous phenomena, it's just not working for me.

There's a few wording errors related to the finer points of the English language still in the article; I'll take a look in a minute and fix up a few things, but you may want to read this over once more and check for small errors.

One last thing:

Soldatesca Cavalleria of Pisa

I really, really wish people would stop doing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comment Abettik,

I did got some feedback on shortening the article and trimming the fat. I cleared what I could, and kept the essential stuff. Thing is, a lot of stuff is essential for this SCP to be contained successfully, being it keter: What to do with B and W in public? What about the captured ones? What to do, when there is a disproportional number of either? What to do with the area during the event? And then: How? etc., etc.

Then again, I wanted the hook to be a bit more implicit than other keters. Bunch of guys fighting, they don't do too much to the world, okay… a few more remaining.. whoa, suddenly the effect is pretty large and could be larger. And then it appears on other (nearby) places, too? Bryx got a pretty good idea how to sum it up above. I remembered the Canada eating fish when writing this one a few times.

I remember our talk at your 2000 proposal and I perfectly respect the different cups of tea here, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, you did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote for now.

This is a little… confusing right now. It's not that I don't understand what's going on, I'm pretty sure I do, though you hint that this is derived from a legend and you also hint at something deeper and more sinister in the [REDACTED] portions, and I've no idea what either could be.

Maybe I'm woozy from my new diet, but other people seem to feel the same way. I guess what I'm saying is that it's difficult to read, and there's not enough payout for it. I don't mind a difficult, overly technical read if the payout is big or if its something I'm really interested in. But this is neither for me, right now. I feel like the containment procedures could be shortened to like three sentences and still make sense, and that a lot of the description could be ironed out. Maybe you should simplify it out, or maybe make the hook a little more obvious, or maybe both.

This is only a gut feeling, so don't take it as gospel. Whenever I see a confusing article, I usual come back and re-read it after having a day or so to think about it. I'll vote + or - then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks again for the review and your opinion, I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Fix'd. I hope that I have made one of your country's legends justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for telling me. It appears Aelanna beat me to it, so I'll keep it in mind next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From the original draft, I thought those tab boxes were part of other drafts so I hadn't read them before now. Honestly, that formatting is a tad bit distracting. Regardless, I still think this is good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please, fix the image's description. It is Fruli-Venezia Giulia, not Fruli-Venezia-Giulia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect is slowly spreading, and if either side has a crushing victory, there is either widespread structural damage from plants or widespread sterility and famine. It's pretty hard to contain that in such a way that something terrible doesn't happen and nobody else finds out about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just too dense here, but I don't get it. There's these people who astral-project, fight each other at night… and that's all. There's some presumably juicy bits which are gratuitously redacted, the structure of the article is extremely awkward with the tabs and multiple collapsibles, and the overall presentation is rather shoddy. Until this starts making more sense, I'm forced to downvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please attach your photos to the current page and link them directly. Some of us can't see the Sandbox due to various content filters, and it makes reading SCPs with pictures a bit frustrating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgot to credit the ppl who helped me, so: Thanks to thedeadlymoose, Jabonicus, Reach, MrCarbon and Azzleflux for their opinions and help with the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You fixed a lot of it, which I like. But the second interview still bugs me. No vote for now.
